title: Pine shadow box frame
subtitle: A shallow display frame for pressed flowers, prints, and small objects
excerpt: A shadow box frame in straight-grained pine: 30 by 20 cm opening, 4 cm deep. Four side rails glued at the corners with simple butt joints, a hardboard back, and an interior chamfer that catches the light. Danish oil on the outside. A weekend project that produces a useful display frame from a few lengths of standard pine.
sourceNotes: Traditional British framing practice; Cassell's Household Guide (1880) vol. II for shadow box dimensions and interior finish.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried timber stabilised at 8 to 12 percent moisture content. Pine for framing should be fully seasoned to prevent warping after the frame is assembled.
A bevelled edge cut at 45 degrees along an interior corner, used in shadow boxes to catch light and add definition to the frame interior.
A vertical cut with the marking knife that defines the boundary of a chamfer and prevents the chisel from tearing past the line.

content.text: A shadow box creates depth between the display surface and the glass; botanical prints, pressed flowers, small objects, and layered arrangements all show better with that 3 to 4 cm of space than in a flat frame. Pine is the standard material: light, easy to work with basic tools, and stable when fully
seasoned
. The construction is simple butt joints at the corners with wood glue; no complex joinery.
The wood

Kiln-dried pine: 4 pieces at 4 cm wide and 4 cm deep, cut to length with a handsaw. For a 30 by 20 cm opening: two side rails at 30 cm, two end rails at 28 cm (the end rails fit inside the side rails for a simple butt joint). A piece of 3 mm hardboard at 32 by 22 cm for the back. Pine off-cuts from a builder's merchant or timber yard work perfectly; no need for a full-length board.
Method
Cut the interior
chamfer
on each rail before assembly; it is much easier to chamfer each piece individually than to work on the assembled box. Use a marking knife to score the
stop cut
lines before any chisel work.

Mark the interior chamfer on each rail: 8 mm in from the interior edge on both the inside face and the back face. Score these lines with the marking knife. The chamfer will run along the full interior edge of each rail; the corner where the interior wall meets the back.

Chisel the chamfer on each rail: set the bench chisel at 45 degrees to the interior edge, bevel down, and take the chamfer in 2 mm passes from one end to the other. Pine is soft enough that hand pressure alone can drive the chisel; a mallet is rarely needed. Keep the chisel flat against the marked surface to produce a consistent 45-degree face.
Sand all interior surfaces through 120 and 180 grit before assembly; it is impossible to sand the interior corners of the assembled box. The exterior faces need only 180 grit.

Apply Danish oil to all surfaces including the interior chamfers before assembly; again, much easier to finish individual pieces. Let the oil set for 24 hours before assembly so the glue surface is clean wood, not oiled.

Assemble the box: apply wood glue to the end grain of the end rails and press the four rails together, checking the corners are at 90 degrees. Clamp or tape with masking tape until the glue sets; 1 to 2 hours for standard PVA. Glue the hardboard back panel to the assembled box from the back face.
Attach a hanging batten to the back: a 25 cm length of the same pine, glued and nailed horizontally across the back, 3 cm from the top. Drill two holes through the batten for wall screws.
Variations
Oak or ash rails: much heavier; the same construction method, but the glue joints need to be tight as there is less grain penetration with hardwood end grain.
Double-depth box: use 8 cm deep rails for a 7 cm interior depth; large enough for small sculptural objects, seed heads, or layered fabric.
Care
Dust the interior with a soft brush. Re-oil the exterior annually. Keep away from direct sunlight; pine darkens unevenly when exposed to prolonged UV.

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
attrs.intro: Common problems with the pine shadow box frame.

items.symptom: Corners not at 90 degrees after gluing
items.cause: Assembly done without checking square before the glue set
items.fix: Check with a set square immediately after clamping. If not square, adjust the clamp angle while the glue is still wet. If the glue has set, cut through the corner joint with a saw, re-square, and re-glue.

items.symptom: Chamfer inconsistent; wider at one end than the other
items.cause: Chisel not held at a consistent angle along the rail length
items.fix: Mark a reference angle with pencil on the end grain and check the chisel against it at the start of each pass. Chamfer in full-length strokes rather than short passes.

items.symptom: Glue joint fails at one corner
items.cause: Oiled surface on the end grain where glue was applied
items.fix: Sand the oiled end grain back to bare wood before re-applying glue. Oil prevents glue penetration into pine end grain.
